elements.sent-2411-001: Recent advancements in machine learning, particularly through deep learning architectures like PointNet, have transformed the processing of three-dimensional (3D) point clouds, significantly improving 3D object classification and segmentation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-002: While 3D point clouds provide detailed spatial information, spatio-temporal signals introduce a dynamic element that accounts for changes over time.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, applying deep learning techniques to spatio-temporal signals and deploying them on edge devices presents challenges, including real-time processing, memory capacity, and power consumption.
elements.sent-2411-004: To address these issues, this paper presents a novel approach that combines PointNet's feature extraction with the in-memory computing capabilities and energy efficiency of neuromorphic systems for spatio-temporal signal recognition.
elements.sent-2411-005: The proposed method consists of a two-stage process: in the first stage, PointNet extracts features from the spatio-temporal signals, which are then stored in non-volatile memristor crossbar arrays.
elements.sent-2411-006: In the second stage, these features are processed by a single-layer spiking neural encoder-decoder that employs the Locally Competitive Algorithm (LCA) for efficient encoding and classification.
elements.sent-2411-007: This work integrates the strengths of both PointNet and LCA, enhancing computational efficiency and energy performance on edge devices.
elements.sent-2411-008: PointLCA-Net achieves high recognition accuracy for spatio-temporal data with substantially lower energy burden during both inference and training than comparable approaches, thus advancing the deployment of advanced neural architectures in energy-constrained environments.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[scale=0.12]{Figures/Figs/LCA-Decoder-3D.png} \caption{PointLCA-Net Architecture: Features ($\phi_i$) are extracted once and stored in the synaptic weights of a single-layer SNN for inference. The orange arrows indicate the inference process that follows the completion of training (feature extraction).} \label{lca-arch}
elements.sent-2411-009: In recent years, the advent of deep learning has significantly advanced the analysis of complex data structures, with architectures such as PointNet~\cite{qi2017pointnet revolutionizing the processing of three-dimensional (3D) point clouds.}
elements.sent-2411-010: PointNet is a powerful tool for extracting invariant features from unordered point cloud data, using a symmetric aggregation mechanism to capture the intrinsic properties of 3D objects.
elements.sent-2411-011: This capability has led to substantial improvements in tasks such as 3D object classification and segmentation~\cite{qi2017pointnet, qi2017pointnet++, ma2022rethinking , where handling irregular and unordered input is crucial.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Despite these advancements, applying such techniques in edge devices —- where energy efficiency is crucial —- remains constrained by the limitations of conventional computing systems on real-time processing of complex data.
elements.sent-2411-013: These limitations include challenges related to data movement, memory capacity, and power consumption.
elements.sent-2411-014: Conventional processors, such as CPUs and GPUs/TPUs, are optimized for handling dense, synchronized data structures through the effective utilization of instruction and data pipelines.
elements.sent-2411-015: However, their efficiency significantly diminishes when managing sparse, asynchronous event streams~\cite{dave2021hardware .}
elements.sent-2411-016: This calls for a departure from traditional von Neumann computing systems.
elements.sent-2411-017: Recently, neuromorphic computing -- drawing inspiration from the human brain's capabilities and/or structures -— has emerged as a transformative approach in computing, offering energy-efficient and massively parallel processing elements~\cite{zheng2019learning .}
elements.sent-2411-018: A new wave of neuromorphic processors~\cite{amir2017low,davies2018loihi,furber2014spinnaker,hoppner2021spinnaker,khaddam2022hermes,le202364,pei2019towards has been developed to deliver low-latency and low-power consumption, making them well-suited for modeling Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) and processing sparse data streams from event-based sensors.}
elements.sent-2411-019: SNNs represent a compelling paradigm for modeling temporal dynamics, providing advantages in processing sequential data through discrete spike events that emulate biological neural activity.
elements.sent-2411-020: Unlike traditional neural networks, SNNs compute with asynchronous spikes that are temporally precise as opposed to continuous-valued activations that operate on a common clock cycle.
elements.sent-2411-021: SNNs have the potential to excel at representing and processing temporal sequences, making them particularly well-suited for tasks involving dynamic patterns and time-varying signals.
elements.sent-2411-022: Neuromorphic chips support neurons that generate asynchronous binary outputs (spikes).
elements.sent-2411-023: They enable the efficient execution of vector-matrix multiplication (VMM) operations, also known as multiply-and-accumulate (MAC) operations, which are fundamental to deep learning algorithms, by leveraging a crossbar array of memory elements~\cite{aguirre2024hardware .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Non-volatile memory elements, such as PCM~\cite{kim2015nvm, kuzum2011nanoelectronic, tuma2016detecting and RRAM~\cite{covi2016analog, pantazi2016all, rao2023thousands, serb2016unsupervised , have been proposed for integration into synapse and neuron circuits~\cite{sebastian2020memory .}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: However, most efforts to apply in-memory computing to SNNs have focused on unsupervised learning using local learning rules, such as Spike-Timing Dependent Plasticity (STDP)~\cite{indiveri2015memory which adjusts a synaptic weight of a neuron based on the relative timing between its inputs and outputs.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Nonetheless, STDP-based learning rules face limited scalability and applicability to complex learning tasks such as 3D object classification or recognizing spatio-temporal signals.
elements.sent-2411-027: This paper presents a novel algorithm (PointLCA-Net) that leverages PointNet for feature extraction and integrates these features with in-memory computing and neuromorphic processing to address the challenges of recognizing spatio-temporal signals on these platforms.
elements.sent-2411-028: These features are extracted once and stored on non-volatile memory elements, allowing for in-memory computation on neuromorphic platforms that prioritize specialized operations and energy-efficient processing.
elements.sent-2411-029: We propose a two-stage approach where spatio-temporal data, such as that from DVS cameras, is initially preprocessed and converted into 3D point cloud data.
elements.sent-2411-030: In the first stage, PointNet generates robust feature representations from this 3D point cloud data.
elements.sent-2411-031: These features, which capture both geometric and semantic information, are then input into a single-layer SNN encoder-decoder architecture in the second stage for classification tasks.
elements.sent-2411-032: Neuromorphic operation on sparse data should likewise exhibit and exploit sparsity.
elements.sent-2411-033: To that end, we consider approaches that employ the Locally Competitive Algorithm (LCA)~\cite{rozell2008sparse as the encoder to ensure that only a limited number of neurons are active at any given time, enabling sparsity and efficient coding of high-dimensional data.}
elements.sent-2411-034: One proposal for working with these sparse LCA encodings is the Exemplar LCA-Decoder~\cite{takaghaj2024exemplar .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Used as a single-layer encoder-decoder, it is a computational model that iteratively updates neuron activity to find a sparse representation of the input data (i.e, encoding) and then uses these neuron activities for classification tasks (i.e, decoding).
elements.sent-2411-036: The objective of this study is to evaluate PointLCA-Net and assess the efficacy of integrating PointNet's feature extraction with the efficiency of sparse coding through LCA in a manner that is deployable on edge devices.
elements.sent-2411-037: By feeding features derived from PointNet into a single-layer SNN model, we aim to achieve high recognition accuracy for spatio-temporal data while maintaining low computational overhead and energy efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-038: In summary, the contribution of this paper is a novel approach to PointNets that integrates the Winner-Take-All mechanism from SNNs.
elements.sent-2411-039: This contribution improves the computational efficiency of PointNets, making them more suitable for deployment on embedded systems.
elements.sent-2411-040: Only a modest number of recent works have studied how to apply SNNs to point clouds, primarily due to the prohibitive computational overhead involved in training.
elements.sent-2411-041: Wang et al.~\cite{wang2019space proposed a method for constructing Space-time Event Clouds (SECs) from event camera data and employed the original PointNet~\cite{qi2017pointnet and the hierarchical PointNet++~\cite{qi2017pointnet++ architectures for gesture recognition tasks.}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Spiking PointNet~\cite{ren2024spiking is the first spiking neural network model designed for efficient deep learning on point clouds.}
elements.sent-2411-043: The model is trained using a single time step, and membrane potential perturbation is introduced to enhance its performance.
elements.sent-2411-044: It offers significant speedup and storage savings during the training phase and, in certain experiments, surpasses its DNN counterpart, demonstrating the potential of SNNs for point cloud tasks.
elements.sent-2411-045: However, using single time-step training limits the model's ability to capture complex temporal dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-046: Additionally, the inherent complexity of training SNNs in this fashion may become prohibitive when applied to more complex datasets beyond the ModelNet10 and ModelNet40 datasets tested in that work.
elements.sent-2411-047: SpikePoint~\cite{renspikepoint employs a SNN architecture specifically designed for event camera action recognition.}
elements.sent-2411-048: The use of surrogate gradients in this approach involves computing the derivative of a surrogate function, which can be computationally expensive, particularly for complex architectures such as PointNet.
elements.sent-2411-049: Furthermore, the accuracy and convergence of training with surrogate gradients is sensitive to the choice of surrogate function's parameters, potentially leading to additional computational costs for tuning and optimization.
elements.sent-2411-050: Fig.~\ref{lca-arch shows the architecture of PointLCA-Net.}
elements.sent-2411-051: The spatio-temporal training data is pre-processed and sent to PointNet for feature extraction.
elements.sent-2411-052: These features are then stored in the synaptic weights of a single-layer spiking neural network with LCA encoding and used for classifying (unseen) test inputs.
elements.sent-2411-053: Fig.~\ref{fig:pointnet illustrates the feature extraction process using the PointNet~\cite{qi2017pointnet architecture, which processes 3D point cloud data by first aligning the data into a canonical space using an input transformation network (Input Transform).}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Each point is then independently processed through shared Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs) and feature transformation network (Feature Transform) to extract point-wise features.
elements.sent-2411-055: These features are aggregated using a symmetric function (Max Pooling), to produce a permutation-invariant global feature vector.
elements.sent-2411-056: The resulting 1024-dimensional feature vector captures comprehensive geometric and semantic information of the point cloud.
elements.sent-2411-057: These features $\phi_i$ are then used to construct the dictionary $\phi$ (Eq.~\ref{dictionary ).}
elements.subsection-2411-002: Single-Layer SNN with LCA Encoder and Decoder
elements.sent-2411-058: We first provide an overview of the Exemplar LCA-Decoder algorithm~\cite{takaghaj2024exemplar , which utilizes the sparse coding algorithm~\cite{rozell2008sparse and the LCA algorithm~\cite{4379981 to represent the input signal $\mathit{S}$: \begin{equation}\label{recon} S = \sum_{i=0}^{M-1} \phi_{i} a_{i} + \varepsilon\end{equation} where $\phi$ is a dictionary of features ($\phi_i$) and $a_{i}$ represents the activation of the LIF neuron $i$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: The LIF neuron's membrane potential $u_i$ is subject to a driving excitatory input $b_i$ and an inhibition matrix (Gramian) $G$.
elements.sent-2411-061: The Gramian matrix allows stronger neurons to prevent weaker neurons from becoming active, resulting in a sparse representation. \begin{equation}\label{neuron update} \tau \dot{u}_i[k]+u_i[k]=b_i-\sum_{m\neq i}^{M-1}G_{i,m}a_m[k]\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{b_i} b_i = S\phi_i\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{grammian} G = \phi^{T}\phi\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{dictionary} \phi = [\phi_0, \phi_1, \ldots, \phi_{M-1}]\end{equation} Each $\phi_i$ represents a feature learned from a data point in the training dataset.
elements.sent-2411-062: This approach eliminates the need for dictionary learning, differing from the original LCA, where Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) is used to learn and update the dictionary for each batch of input data.
elements.sent-2411-063: The thresholding function is: \begin{equation}\label{thresholding} a_i[k] = T_\lambda(u_i[k]) \nonumber =  u_i[k] - \lambda \operatorname{sign}(u_i[k]), \quad \left| u_i[k] \right| \geq \lambda 0, \quad \left| u_i[k] \right| < \lambda \end{equation} Here, the threshold $\lambda$ refers to the level that the membrane potential must exceed for the neuron to become active.
elements.sent-2411-064: Next, a decoder is designed to decode the sparse codes $a_i$ and map them to K distinct classes from the training dataset.
elements.sent-2411-065: Later, the same decoder is used to generate predictions or classifications for the unseen test data points.
elements.sent-2411-066: Given a sufficient number of training data points M, each test data point will have a sparse coding representation $a = [a_0, a_1, a_2, ..., a_{M-1 ]$, where the majority of the $a_{i}$ values will be zero.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Ideally, for any test input $S_{Test}$, the non-zero entries in the vector $a$ would correspond exclusively to the dictionary atoms $\phi_i$ associated with class k, where k ranges from 1 to K.
elements.sent-2411-068: However, modeling errors and input signal noise may introduce small non-zero entries that are associated with multiple classes.
elements.sent-2411-069: To address this issue and better harness the relevance of neuron activations for each class, the ``Maximum Sum of Activations'' decoder is proposed.
elements.sent-2411-070: In this approach, the $\ell_1$ norms of the activations for each class k are summed and the class with the highest value is determined: \begin{equation}\label{max-sum-decoder} Predicted \; Class = \underset{k}{argmax}(\sum_i \left | a_{i}^{(k)}\right |)\end{equation}.
elements.figure-2411-003: \caption{Top-1 Test Accuracy Scores for PointLCA-Net.} \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \multicolumn{3}{c}{} \multicolumn{1}{c}{} \multicolumn{2}{|c|}{Accuracy} \multicolumn{3}{c}{} \multicolumn{1}{c}{} \multicolumn{2}{|c|}{Decoding Methods} \textbf {Dataset} Method GFLOPs Energy \textbf {$\underset{i}{max} \left\{ a_{i} \right\}$} \textbf {$\underset{k}{max} \sum_{i} \left | a_{i}^{(k)}\right |$} NMNIST PointLCA-Net 3 0.27 mJ 95.49\% 98.01\% DVS128 PointLCA-Net 0.7 0.065 mJ 82.67\% 90.02\% SHD PointLCA-Net 0.07 $6.4 \, \mu$J 59.85\% 65.11\% DVS128 SpikePoint~\cite{renspikepoint}$^{\mathrm{a}}$ 0.9 0.82 mJ \multicolumn{2}{c|}{98.6\%} \multicolumn{6}{l}{$^{\mathrm{a}}$This method hasn't been tested on NMNIST or SHD.} \end{tabular} \label{tab:decoders}
elements.figure-2411-004: \caption{Hyperparameters} \label{parameter} \begin{center} \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|} Symbols Description Value $\lambda$ Threshold 0.2 $\tau$ Leakage 1000 $k$ Number of time steps 100 \end{tabular} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-071: We evaluated our work using PyTorch~\cite{pytorch and the following datasets: NMNIST~\cite{orchard , DVS128~\cite{amir2017low and Spiking Heidelberg Digits (SHD)~\cite{cramer2020heidelberg .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: Event streams in these spatio-temporal datasets are treated as space-time event clouds, where each event is represented by its spatial coordinates (x, y) and temporal component (t).
elements.sent-2411-073: We concatenate theses coordinates into a tensor of shape (x, y, t) for processing.
elements.sent-2411-074: Due to the temporal variability inherent in these datasets, we preprocess the data by standardizing the input size to ensure uniformity for training and evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-075: The preprocessing phase begins by evaluating the duration of the events.
elements.sent-2411-076: Based on this assessment, the data is segmented into smaller windows, with the sizes and numbers of these windows determined by the event duration.
elements.sent-2411-077: Each window is then sampled to select a fixed number of events (128, 256, 512, or 1024).
elements.sent-2411-078: These sampled events are concatenated, shuffled and downsampled to create a final subset of 1024 events, which is subsequently input into the PointNet for feature extraction.
elements.sent-2411-079: The NMNIST dataset is a spiking variant of the MNIST dataset, generated by presenting images to a neuromorphic vision camera equipped with the ATIS (Asynchronous Time-based Image Sensor).
elements.sent-2411-080: This dataset comprises 60,000 training images, each consisting of 300 time samples, along with 10,000 test images used for accuracy evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-081: The DVS128 dataset, captured using a Dynamic Vision Sensor (DVS) camera, includes recordings of 11 distinct hand gestures, with approximately 20,000 to 40,000 events per gesture class.
elements.sent-2411-082: It contains 1,176 samples allocated for training and 288 samples for testing.
elements.sent-2411-083: The SHD dataset comprises spoken digits from zero to nine in both English and German.
elements.sent-2411-084: Audio waveforms within the dataset are transformed into spike trains using an artificial cochlea model.
elements.sent-2411-085: The dataset includes 8,156 training samples and 2,264 test samples, each with varying temporal duration.
elements.sent-2411-086: Table~\ref{tab:decoders displays the energy estimation and accuracy performance across all datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-087: All experiments were consistently conducted using the hyperparameters specified in Table~\ref{parameter .}
elements.sent-2411-088: These hyperparameters were selected to achieve near 100\% training accuracy, with the results demonstrating the test accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-089: Further tuning of these hyperparameters could potentially enhance accuracy for each dataset even further.
elements.sent-2411-090: Additionally, Table~\ref{tab:decoders shows that the ``Maximum Sum of Activations'' decoder yields significantly improved results across all datasets compared to the ``Maximum Activation'' decoder.}
elements.sent-2411-091: The energy estimation was calculated based on the computed floating point operations during inference (Table~\ref{flops ) and the expected energy consumption per floating point operation, which is discussed in detail in the following section.}
elements.sent-2411-092: In this section, we conduct a comprehensive evaluation of the workload and energy efficiency of PointLCA-Net.
elements.sent-2411-093: We assess the workload efficiency by estimating the number of Floating-Point Operations (FLOPs) and follow this with a discussion of energy consumption.
elements.sent-2411-094: Computing $b_i$ in Eq.~\ref{b_i requires $N*M$ multiplications and $(N-1)*M$ addition, where $N$ is the global feature size and $M$ represents the length of the dictionary (also the number of spiking neurons).}
elements.sent-2411-095: The inhibition signal calculation in Eq.~\ref{neuron update involves $M^2-M$ multiplications and $M^2 -2M$ additions.}
elements.sent-2411-096: Additionally, the leakage term introduces $M$ multiplication operations, while $3M$ additions are required to combine these terms and update the neurons' membrane potentials, as specified in Eq.~\ref{neuron update .}
elements.sent-2411-097: Furthermore, computing the Gramian matrix $G$ in Eq.\ref{grammian entails $\frac{M(M+1)N {2}$ multiplications and $\frac{M(M+1)(N-1) {2}$ additions. \begin{equation}\label{FLOPS-training} \underset{(Training)}{FLOPs} = \frac{M(M+1)(2N-1)}{2}\end{equation} This computation, considered part of the training cost and performed once per task (dataset), is excluded from the inference cost.}}}
elements.sent-2411-098: Thus, the total floating-point operations required per time step $K$ for inference is given by: \begin{equation}\label{FLOPS1} \underset{(Inferenec)}{FLOPs} = K(\frac{(2N-1)M}{K}+2M^2+M)\end{equation} Note that $b_i$ is computed once per input data and remains constant across iterations.
elements.sent-2411-099: Finally, the expected sparsity through LCA is factored in by redefining $M$, the number of active neurons, as $\hat{M}$, which represents the average number of firing neurons whose $a_m \neq 0$. \begin{equation}\label{FLOPS2} \underset{(Inferenec)}{FLOPs} = K(\frac{(2N-1)M}{K}+2M\hat{M}+M)\end{equation}.
elements.figure-2411-005: \caption{Workload analysis of PointLCA-Net} \label{flops} \begin{tabular}{|l|>{ \arraybackslash}m{1.1cm}|l|l|l|l|>{ \arraybackslash}m{1.1cm}|} Dataset Training (TFLOPs) K N M $\bm{\hat{M}}$$^{\mathrm{a}}$ Inference (GFLOPs) \multirow{2}{*}{NMNIST} \multirow{2}{*} {3.7} 100 1024 60K 240 3 10 1024 60K 240 0.4 \multirow{2}{*}{DVS128} \multirow{2}{*} {0.84} 100 1024 28.6K 114 0.7 10 1024 28.6K 114 0.12 \multirow{2}{*}{SHD} \multirow{2}{*} {0.07} 100 1024 8.156K 33 0.07 10 1024 8.156K 33 0.02 \multicolumn{7}{l}{$^{\mathrm{a}}$ From~\cite{takaghaj2024exemplar}, we assumed that 0.4\% of neurons spike at each time step.} \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-100: Table~\ref{flops displays the estimated training and inference FLOPs for PointLCA-Net.}
elements.sent-2411-101: Training FLOPs accounts for all training data points, whereas inference FLOPs is estimated for a single test input.
elements.sent-2411-102: We conducted two scenarios with 100 and 10 time steps (K).
elements.sent-2411-103: Reducing the number of time steps from 100 to 10 resulted in an average reduction of 80\% in inference GFLOPs.
elements.sent-2411-104: Additionally, the sparsity induced by LCA led to an average reduction of 99.54\% in computational effort during inference.
elements.sent-2411-105: In addition to the improvement in workload efficiency, PointLCA-Net leverages recent advancements in in-memory computing through memristive crossbar arrays.
elements.sent-2411-106: Memristive crossbars can perform multiplication operations based on Ohm’s Law (I = V · G, where I is the current, V is the input voltage, and G is the conductance of each memristor), thereby enhancing energy efficiency and reducing the area footprint.
elements.sent-2411-107: Yao et al.~\cite{yao2020fully reported an energy efficiency of 11 Tera FLOPs per Watt for MAC operations using RRAM crossbar arrays.}
elements.sent-2411-108: This is equivalent to each floating point operation consuming approximately $9.09 \times 10^{-14}$ joules of energy.
elements.sent-2411-109: Fig.~\ref{fig:p illustrates the hardware mapping of PointLCA-Net, where the $\phi_{i}$ values are represented as the conductance of memristors in each column and are used for neuron excitatory input multiplications in Eq.~\ref{b_i .}}
elements.sent-2411-110: Inputs are preprocessed in the input peripheral circuits, while neuron dynamics and thresholding (as defined in Eq.~\ref{neuron update and Eq.~\ref{thresholding ) are implemented in the output peripheral circuits in the neural processor.}}
elements.sent-2411-111: The calculation of the Gramian matrix, which serves as the training head in this algorithm, can be performed offline, with the results stored in a lookup table (LUT) in the on-chip memory of the host processor.
elements.sent-2411-112: We successfully evaluated PointLCA-Net on spatio-temporal data using the NMNIST, DVS128, and SHD datasets.
elements.sent-2411-113: While this work marks the first application of PointNet to NMNIST and SHD, SpikePoint~\cite{renspikepoint , a variant of PointNets designed for SNNs, has been tested on the DVS128 dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-114: SpikePoint utilizes a surrogate training method, achieving an accuracy of 98.6\% with a GFLOPs value of 0.9, compared to 0.7 for PointLCA-Net.
elements.sent-2411-115: However, its estimated dynamic energy consumption is 0.82 mJ, approximately 12.62 times greater than that of PointLCA-Net.
elements.sent-2411-116: Additionally, Wang et al.~\cite{wang2019space reported an accuracy of 90.20\% on the DVS128 dataset with 10 classes using the original PointNet, and 97.08\% with PointNet++ through a proposed rolling buffer framework.}
elements.sent-2411-117: Although this work was the first to apply PointNet to spatio-temporal data, it did not address hardware deployment, leaving the energy efficiency of the approach unknown.
elements.sent-2411-118: This study serves as a proof of concept and has significant potential as an algorithm that can be uniformly applied to various spatio-temporal data, including video and audio.
elements.sent-2411-119: However, it has limitations in accuracy compared to the state of the art, and further improvements could be achieved by exploring alternative architectures for feature extraction, such as PointNet++~\cite{qi2017pointnet++ and Point Transformer~\cite{zhao2021point .}}
elements.sent-2411-120: PointNet++ is a hierarchical extension of PointNet that applies the architecture recursively to a nested partitioning of the input set and adaptively combines features from multiple scales.
elements.sent-2411-121: Unlike PointNet's single-stage processing that aggregates features globally with simpler operations, PointNet++ processes points in local neighborhoods, performing additional clustering and multi-scale feature extraction.
elements.sent-2411-122: This hierarchical approach increases the number of operations and memory usage, leading to a higher computational load.
elements.sent-2411-123: While we didn't explore using PointNet++ features in this work, it is an area of interest for future research.
elements.sent-2411-124: Finally, in this study, we utilized the entire set of training data points to construct the dictionary.
elements.sent-2411-125: While larger dictionary lengths can improve accuracy, they may also create a computational burden for large datasets.
elements.sent-2411-126: To enhance computational efficiency, one potential approach is to reduce the dictionary length \( M \) through Principal Component Analysis (PCA), Support Vector Machines (SVM), or by selecting discriminative features, thereby decreasing the computational overhead.
elements.sent-2411-127: For instance, a 10\% reduction in dictionary length could, on average, reduce inference FLOPs by 17\% and training FLOPs by 20\%.
elements.sent-2411-128: As a result, this opens up new avenues for enhancing computational efficiency and model performance in future studies.
elements.sent-2411-129: In this work, we explored integrating LCA with PointNets for the first time and assessed its performance on classification tasks using the NMNIST, DVS128, and SHD datasets.
elements.sent-2411-130: We utilized features extracted through PointNet within an LCA encoder-decoder framework, which eliminated the need for training a dictionary as done in the original LCA.
elements.sent-2411-131: Additionally, we demonstrated how this approach can be mapped to memristor crossbar arrays for efficient in-memory computing on edge devices.
elements.sent-2411-132: This study highlights the potential for further research, including the investigation of alternative architectures such as PointNet++ and Point Transformer for higher accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-133: Given the energy efficiency potential of PointLCA-Net and its adaptability to various datasets, it presents an opportunity for enhancement in spatio-temporal data classification.
elements.sent-2411-134: In recent years, the field of neuromorphic systems has gained significant traction as the demand for AI applications in energy-constrained autonomous systems, such as unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) and robotics, continues to grow.
elements.sent-2411-135: The advent of Dynamic Vision Sensor (DVS) and other event-based sensors, which generate sparse spatio-temporal signals, has propelled the field of neuromorphic engineering.
elements.sent-2411-136: This advancement calls for the development of new machine learning models or the refinement of existing ones to effectively leverage neural dynamics and exploit spike-based communication among neurons.
elements.sent-2411-137: These sparse spatio-temporal signals can be treated as 3D point sets (point clouds), representing the space and time at which these events occur, and can be processed using PointNet architectures.
elements.sent-2411-138: However, applying deep learning techniques such as backpropagation and deploying these models on energy-constrained neuromorphic devices presents challenges in computational resources and memory, as well as real-time processing and power consumption.
elements.sent-2411-139: This work presents PointLCA-Net, which leverages the strengths of PointNets for extracting robust features from input point sets, while utilizing the efficiency of Exemplar LCA to encode these features.
elements.sent-2411-140: PointLCA-Net achieves a high accuracy of 93.41\% on DVS128 while reducing energy consumption by approximately 92\% compared to other spiking neural networks applied to point clouds, making the system more efficient and well-suited for energy-constrained applications.
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[scale=0.12]{Figures/Figs/LCA-Decoder-3D.png} \caption{PointLCA-Net Architecture: Features ($\phi_i$) are extracted from the training data and stored in the synaptic weights of the Exemplar LCA-Decoder. The orange arrows represent the inference process, which follows the completion of training (feature extraction).} \label{lca-arch}
elements.sent-2411-141: Deep learning has significantly advanced the analysis of complex data structures, with architectures such as PointNet~\cite{qi2017pointnet and PointNet++~\cite{qi2017pointnet++ revolutionizing the processing of three-dimensional (3D) point sets (point clouds).}}
elements.sent-2411-142: These architectures have led to substantial improvements in tasks such as 3D object classification and segmentation~\cite{qi2017pointnet, qi2017pointnet++, ma2022rethinking .}
elements.sent-2411-143: PointNet takes point clouds as input, applies input and feature transformations, and aggregates the point features using a max pooling function.
elements.sent-2411-144: The network outputs a set of classification scores for \( k \) classes, along with segmentation scores.
elements.sent-2411-145: PointNet++ is a hierarchical extension of PointNet that applies the architecture recursively to a nested partitioning of the input set and adaptively combines features from multiple scales to enhance the generalizability of PointNet to complex 3D shapes and point cloud structures.
elements.sent-2411-146: Unlike PointNet, which processes points globally, PointNet++ processes points within local neighborhoods, performing additional clustering and multi-scale feature extraction.
elements.sent-2411-147: Point clouds can represent discrete spatio-temporal signals by capturing both spatial and temporal information as discrete points in space-time.
elements.sent-2411-148: Wang et al.~\cite{wang2019space proposed a method for constructing Space-time Event Clouds from Dynamic Vision Sensors (DVSs) and applied the PointNet and PointNet++ architectures to gesture recognition task on DVS128~\cite{amir2017low .}}
elements.sent-2411-149: Despite these advancements, applying such techniques on edge devices — where energy efficiency is crucial — remains challenging due to the limitations of conventional computing systems.
elements.sent-2411-150: These challenges include issues related to data movement, memory capacity, and power consumption.
elements.sent-2411-151: Conventional processors, such as CPUs and GPUs/TPUs, are optimized for handling dense, synchronized data structures through efficient instruction and data pipelines.
elements.sent-2411-152: However, their performance significantly deteriorates when dealing with sparse, asynchronous event streams~\cite{dave2021hardware .}
elements.sent-2411-153: This calls for a departure from traditional von Neumann computing systems, with neuromorphic computing serving as one potential solution.
elements.sent-2411-154: Recently, neuromorphic systems -- drawing inspiration from the human brain's capabilities and/or structures - have emerged as a transformative approach in computing systems, offering energy-efficient and massively parallel processing elements.
elements.sent-2411-155: A new wave of neuromorphic processors including TrueNorth~\cite{merolla2014million, amir2017low , Loihi~\cite{davies2018loihi, davies2021advancing , SpiNNaker~\cite{furber2014spinnaker, furber2016large, hoppner2021spinnaker , BrainScaleS~\cite{schmitt2017neuromorphic,pehle2022brainscales , Tianjic~\cite{pei2019towards , and DYNAP-SE~\cite{richter2024dynap has been developed to deliver low-latency and low-power consumption, making them well-suited for modeling Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs)~\cite{ghosh2009spiking, gruning2014spiking and processing sparse data streams from event-based sensors.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-156: Neuromorphic systems can efficiently execute Vector-Matrix Multiplication (VMM) operations, also known as Multiply-and-Accumulate (MAC) operations, by leveraging a crossbar array of memory elements~\cite{aguirre2024hardware , which are fundamental to deep learning algorithms.}
elements.sent-2411-157: Non-volatile memory elements, such as PCM~\cite{kim2015nvm, kuzum2012nanoelectronic, tuma2016detecting and RRAM~\cite{covi2016analog, pantazi2016all, rao2023thousands, serb2016unsupervised , have been proposed for integration into synapse and neuron circuits~\cite{sebastian2020memory .}}}
elements.sent-2411-158: While most efforts to apply in-memory computing to SNNs have focused on unsupervised learning with local learning rules, such as Spike-Timing Dependent Plasticity (STDP)~\cite{indiveri2015memory , which adjusts synaptic weights based on the relative timing between inputs and outputs, these approaches encounter significant challenges when applied to complex datasets and tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-159: STDP-based learning rules have limited scalability and are not well-suited for more complex tasks, such as recognizing spatio-temporal signals.
elements.sent-2411-160: As a result, there is a clear need for new learning rules that can handle dynamic data and address these complex challenges.
elements.sent-2411-161: A particularly interesting model in neuromorphic computing is the Exemplar LCA-Decoder\cite{mahmoodi2024d, takaghaj2024vit , which builds upon the Locally Competitive Algorithm (LCA)\cite{4379981, rozell2008sparse .}}
elements.sent-2411-162: The Exemplar LCA-Decoder is a computational model and learning algorithm that iteratively updates neuron activity to achieve a sparse representation of input data.
elements.sent-2411-163: As a single-layer encoder-decoder architecture, it first encodes the input data features by identifying a sparse representation through neuron activity and then uses this sparse representation for classification tasks during the decoding phase.
elements.sent-2411-164: A key component of this algorithm is the construction of a comprehensive dictionary of input data features.
elements.sent-2411-165: This work builds upon the foundational principles of the Exemplar LCA-Decoder and introduces a novel algorithm, PointLCA-Net, to address the challenges of recognizing spatio-temporal signals on neuromorphic platforms.
elements.sent-2411-166: We propose a two-stage approach in which spatio-temporal data, such as that from DVS cameras, is initially preprocessed and converted into point clouds.
elements.sent-2411-167: In the first stage, a pre-trained PointNet generates robust feature representations from this 3D point cloud data.
elements.sent-2411-168: These features, which capture both geometric and semantic information, are then input into the Exemplar LCA-Decoder architecture in the second stage for encoding and decoding.
elements.sent-2411-169: The Locally Competitive Algorithm (LCA)~\cite{rozell2008sparse is used as the encoder to ensure that only a limited number of neurons are active at any given time, enabling sparsity and efficient coding of data.}
elements.sent-2411-170: We evaluated PointLCA-Net on three datasets: NMNIST~\cite{orchard , DVS128~\cite{amir2017low , and Spiking Heidelberg Digits (SHD)~\cite{cramer2020heidelberg , demonstrating how it can be uniformly applied to each of these datasets in a way that is deployable on neuromorphic platforms.}}}
elements.sent-2411-171: We achieved high recognition accuracy across all these datasets while maintaining low computational overhead and energy efficiency, making the approach well-suited for deployment on neuromorphic systems.
elements.sent-2411-172: The contributions of this work include the following:.
elements.sent-2411-173: A neuromorphic and energy-efficient adaptation of the PointNet and PointNet++ models.
elements.sent-2411-174: First application of the event cloud approach to neuromorphic datasets NMNIST and SHD.
elements.sent-2411-175: A proof of concept for an algorithm that can be uniformly applied to various spatio-temporal datasets for energy-efficient signal recognition on neuromorphic hardware.
elements.sent-2411-176: Spiking PointNet~\cite{ren2024spiking is the first spiking version of PointNet, which leverages spiking neurons.}
elements.sent-2411-177: The model is trained using a single time step, with membrane potential perturbation introduced to improve its performance.
elements.sent-2411-178: It provides some speedup and storage savings during the training phase and, in certain experiments, surpasses its ANN counterpart, suggesting the potential of SNNs for point cloud tasks.
elements.sent-2411-179: However, training with a single time step limits the model's ability to capture complex temporal dynamics, restricting its generalizability to new datasets.
elements.sent-2411-180: Additionally, the inherent complexity of training SNNs in this manner may become prohibitive when applied to more complex datasets beyond the ModelNet10 and ModelNet40 datasets, which are the only ones tested in the Spiking PointNet work.
elements.sent-2411-181: Unlike the structured 3D object data in the ModelNet datasets, DVS128 consists of unstructured spatio-temporal event data, which presents a different set of challenges.
elements.sent-2411-182: This method has not been evaluated on the DVS128 or other datasets considered in our work.
elements.sent-2411-183: SpikePoint~\cite{renspikepoint uses the PointNet++ architecture and converts point coordinates into spikes via rate coding.}
elements.sent-2411-184: It employs surrogate backpropagation for training the network and has primarily been tested on event camera action recognition datasets.
elements.sent-2411-185: The use of surrogate gradients in this approach involves computing the derivative of a surrogate function, which can be computationally expensive, particularly for complex architectures such as PointNet++.
elements.sent-2411-186: The accuracy and convergence of training with surrogate gradients is also sensitive to the choice of surrogate function's parameters, potentially leading to additional computational costs for tuning and optimization.
elements.sent-2411-187: Fig.~\ref{lca-arch illustrates the architecture of PointLCA-Net.}
elements.sent-2411-188: The training dataset is first converted into point clouds consisting of (x, y, t) point sets, pre-processed, and then fed into PointNet for feature extraction.
elements.sent-2411-189: These features are used to construct a dictionary, which is utilized by the Exemplar LCA-Decoder~\cite{mahmoodi2024d to encode and then decode unseen test point clouds.}
elements.sent-2411-190: Fig.~\ref{fig:pointnet illustrates the feature extraction process using a pre-trained PointNet~\cite{qi2017pointnet , which processes point clouds by first aligning the input data points into a canonical space using an input transformation network (Input Transform).}}
elements.sent-2411-191: Each data point is then independently processed through a shared Multi-Layer Perceptron MLP(64, 64), feature transformation network (Feature Transform) and MLP(64, 128, 1024) to extract point-wise features.
elements.sent-2411-192: These features are aggregated using a symmetric Max Pooling function, to produce a permutation-invariant global feature vector $\phi_i$.
elements.sent-2411-193: The resulting 1024-dimensional feature vector captures comprehensive geometric and semantic information from the point cloud.
elements.sent-2411-194: These feature vectors are then used to construct the dictionary $\phi$ (see Fig.~\ref{lca-arch ), which is later used by the Exemplar LCA-Decoder algorithm (see Eq.~\ref{dictionary )).}}
elements.sent-2411-195: The Exemplar LCA-Decoder algorithm~\cite{mahmoodi2024d combines the sparse coding algorithm~\cite{rozell2008sparse with the LCA algorithm~\cite{4379981 to encode the input signal $\mathit{S}$ as the activations $a_{i}$ of the Leaky Integrate and Fire (LIF) neurons where $\phi$ denotes a dictionary of features ($\phi_i$).}}}
elements.sent-2411-196: The term $\varepsilon$ accounts for Gaussian noise in the reconstruction: \begin{equation}\label{recon} S = \sum_{i=0}^{M-1} \phi_{i} a_{i} + \varepsilon\end{equation} The membrane potential of the LIF neuron at time step $k$, denoted as $u_i[k]$, is influenced by a driving excitatory input $b_i$ and an inhibition matrix (Gramian) $G$.
elements.sent-2411-197: The Gramian matrix enables stronger neurons to suppress weaker ones, thereby promoting a sparse coding. \begin{equation}\label{neuron update} \tau \dot{u}_i[k]+u_i[k]=b_i-\sum_{m\neq i}^{M-1}G_{i,m}a_m[k]\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{b_i} b_i = S\phi_i\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{grammian} G = \phi^{T}\phi\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{dictionary} \phi = [\phi_0, \phi_1, \ldots, \phi_{M-1}]\end{equation} In PointLCA-Net, each $\phi_i$ represents a feature learned from a training point cloud.
elements.sent-2411-198: The thresholding function is: \begin{equation}\label{thresholding} a_i[k] = T_\lambda(u_i[k]) \nonumber =  u_i[k] - \lambda \operatorname{sign}(u_i[k]), \quad \left| u_i[k] \right| \geq \lambda 0, \quad \left| u_i[k] \right| < \lambda \end{equation} Here, the threshold $\lambda$ represents the level the membrane potential must exceed for the neuron to spike.
elements.sent-2411-199: This same decoder is then used to generate classifications for unseen test point clouds.
elements.sent-2411-200: The Maximum Activation Code (Eq.\ref{max-decoder ) and Maximum Sum of Activation Codes (Eq.\ref{max-sum-decoder ) were used as decoders for classification.}}
elements.sent-2411-201: In contrast to a shallow (single layer) neural network decoder, which requires additional training and error backpropagation, the Maximum Activation Code and Maximum Sum of Activation Codes offer computational advantages and greater efficiency. \begin{equation}\label{max-decoder} C_{Test} = Class[\underset{i}{argmax} [a_{i}]],\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{max-sum-decoder} C_{Test} = Class[\underset{c}{argmax} (\sum_i \left | a_{i}^{(c)}\right |)]\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-202: We evaluated PointLCA-Net using PyTorch~\cite{pytorch on the following datasets: NMNIST, DVS128 and Spiking Heidelberg Digits (SHD).}
elements.sent-2411-203: Events in these discrete spatio-temporal datasets were converted into point clouds, with each event represented by its spatial coordinates (x, y) and temporal component (t).
elements.sent-2411-204: We then concatenate these coordinates into a tensor of shape (x, y, t) for processing.
elements.sent-2411-205: The NMNIST~\cite{orchard dataset is a spiking variant of the MNIST dataset, generated by presenting images to a neuromorphic vision camera equipped with the ATIS (Asynchronous Time-based Image Sensor).}
elements.sent-2411-206: The SHD~\cite{cramer2020heidelberg dataset comprises spoken digits from zero to nine in both English and German.}
elements.sent-2411-207: The dataset includes 8,156 training samples and 2,264 test samples, each with a varying temporal duration.
elements.sent-2411-208: The DVS128~\cite{amir2017low dataset, captured using a Dynamic Vision Sensor (DVS) camera, includes recordings of 11 distinct hand gestures, with approximately 20,000 to 40,000 events per gesture class.}
elements.sent-2411-209: These samples are further pre-processed, resulting in 28,606 training samples and 7,408 test samples.
elements.sent-2411-210: In our experiments, we included all 11 classes, including the random gesture class.
elements.sent-2411-211: The preprocessing phase begins by evaluating the duration of the recordings.
elements.sent-2411-212: To account for the temporal variability in the dataset, we segment the data into sliding windows, with both the sizes and number of these windows determined based on the total duration of the recordings (see Fig.~\ref{preprocessing ).}
elements.sent-2411-213: The data within each window is then shuffled and down-sampled to select a fixed number of events (128, 256, 512, or 1024).
elements.sent-2411-214: These sampled events are further concatenated, shuffled, and down-sampled, and normalized to create a final subset of 1024 events per data, which is subsequently input into the PointNets for feature extraction.
elements.sent-2411-215: This section presents a thorough evaluation of the workload and energy efficiency of PointLCA-Net.
elements.sent-2411-216: We begin by estimating the number of Floating-Point Operations (FLOPs) to assess workload efficiency, followed by an analysis of its energy consumption.
elements.sent-2411-217: These estimations exclude the FLOPs required for feature extraction by PointNet and PointNet++.
elements.sent-2411-218: Let $N$ represent the global feature size, $M$ the dictionary size (which also corresponds to the number of neurons), and $K$ the total number of time steps.
elements.sent-2411-219: The number of active neurons, denoted as $\hat{M}$, represents the average number of firing neurons for which $a_i \neq 0$.
elements.sent-2411-220: The training and inference FLOPs are then calculated using the approach outlined in \cite{mahmoodi2024d and \cite{takaghaj2024vit : \begin{equation}\label{FLOPS-training} \underset{(Training)}{FLOPs} = \frac{M(M+1)(2N-1)}{2}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{FLOPS2} \underset{(Inferenec)}{FLOPs} = K(\frac{(2N-1)M}{K}+2M\hat{M}+M)\end{equation}.}}

elements.figure-2411-012: \caption{Top-1 Test Accuracy Scores for PointLCA-Net.} \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \multicolumn{3}{c}{} \multicolumn{2}{c}{} \multicolumn{2}{|c|}{Accuracy} \multicolumn{3}{c}{} \multicolumn{2}{c}{} \multicolumn{2}{|c|}{Decoding Methods} \textbf {Dataset} Method GFLOPs Energy \textbf {Feature Extractor} \textbf {$\underset{i}{max} \left\{ a_{i} \right\}$} \textbf {$\underset{k}{max} \sum_{i} \left | a_{i}^{(k)}\right |$} NMNIST PointLCA-Net 3 0.27 mJ PointNet 95.49\% 98.01\% PointNet++ 94.74\% 98.78\% SHD PointLCA-Net 0.07 $6.4 \, \mu$J PointNet 59.85\% 65.11\% PointNet++ 66.30\% 78.46\% DVS128 PointLCA-Net 0.7 0.065 mJ PointNet 82.67\% 90.02\% PointNet++ 86.69\% 93.41\% DVS128 PointNet~\cite{wang2019space}$^{\mathrm{a}}$ - - - \multicolumn{2}{c|}{90.20\%$^{\mathrm{c}}$} PointNet++~\cite{wang2019space}$^{\mathrm{a}}$ 0.872$^{\mathrm{b}}$ 4.01 mJ (Dynamic) and - \multicolumn{2}{c|}{94.10\%} 1.936 mJ (Static)$^{\mathrm{b}}$ \multicolumn{2}{c|}{} DVS128 SpikePoint~\cite{renspikepoint}$^{\mathrm{a}}$ 0.9 0.82 mJ (Dynamic) and - \multicolumn{2}{c|}{98.1\%$^{\mathrm{d}}$} 0.756 mJ (Static) \multicolumn{2}{c|}{} \multicolumn{7}{l}{$^{\mathrm{a}}$These methods haven't been tested on NMNIST or SHD.} \multicolumn{7}{l}{$^{\mathrm{b}}$ The values are taken from the results reported in~\cite{renspikepoint}.} \multicolumn{7}{l}{$^{\mathrm{c}}$ This accuracy was achieved using 10 classes, excluding the random gestures class.} \multicolumn{7}{l}{$^{\mathrm{d}}$This accuracy was achieved using a different data partitioning and sample size than ours.} \end{tabular} \label{tab:decoders}

elements.sent-2411-221: Table~\ref{flops presents the estimated training and inference FLOPs for PointLCA-Net.}
elements.sent-2411-222: According to \cite{mahmoodi2024d , up to 0.4\% of neurons spike at each time step, and we have verified that this estimate is accurate.}
elements.sent-2411-223: The spiking sparsity introduced by LCA encoding resulted in an average reduction of 99.54\% in computational effort during inference.
elements.sent-2411-224: Furthermore, we explored two scenarios with 100 and 10 time steps (K).
elements.sent-2411-225: Reducing the number of time steps from 100 to 10 led to an average decrease of 80\% in inference FLOPs.
elements.sent-2411-226: The neuromorphic-friendly architecture of the Exemplar LCA-Decoder supports the mapping of PointLCA-Net to low-energy neuromorphic platforms, enabling the use of energy-efficient in-memory computing.
elements.sent-2411-227: Using RRAM crossbar arrays, Yao et al.~\cite{yao2020fully demonstrated an energy efficiency of 11 Tera FLOPs per watt for MAC operations, which corresponds to an energy consumption of approximately \( 9.09 \times 10^{-14 \) joules per floating-point operation.}}
elements.sent-2411-228: Beyond improving workload efficiency through sparse coding, PointLCA-Net can also benefit from recent advancements in in-memory computing~\cite{sebastian2020memory and memristive crossbar arrays to further enhance energy efficiency.}
elements.sent-2411-229: Mahmoodi et al.~\cite{takaghaj2024vit proposed a hardware implementation of the Exemplar LCA-Decoder algorithm in ViT-LCA.}
elements.sent-2411-230: Similarly, Fig.\ref{fig:p depicts the hardware mapping of PointLCA-Net.}
elements.sent-2411-231: In this architecture, the values of $\phi_{i}$ are represented as the conductance of memristors in each column, which are used to compute the neuron excitatory inputs, as described in Eq.\ref{b_i .}
elements.sent-2411-232: Input data is preprocessed in the input peripheral circuits, while neuron dynamics and thresholding (defined in Eq.\ref{neuron update and Eq.\ref{thresholding ) are handled in the output peripheral circuits of the neural processor.}}
elements.sent-2411-233: Table~\ref{tab:decoders presents the energy estimations and test accuracy for all three datasets tested.}
elements.sent-2411-234: All experiments were conducted using the hyperparameters specified in Table~\ref{parameter , selected to achieve an accuracy near 100\% when tested on the training data.}
elements.sent-2411-235: The results in Table~\ref{tab:decoders indicate that the ``Maximum Sum of Activations" decoder outperforms the ``Maximum Activation" decoder, delivering significantly better performance across all datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-236: Additionally, using PointNet++ for feature extraction yielded better results than PointNet.
elements.sent-2411-237: The energy estimation was calculated based on the estimated GFLOPs during inference (Table~\ref{flops ) and the expected $9.09 \times 10^{-14}$ joules energy consumption per floating point operation when the operations are mapped onto the RRAM memory crossbar arrays.}
elements.sent-2411-238: Wang et al.~\cite{wang2019space reported an accuracy of 90.20\% on the DVS128 dataset with 10 classes when trained directly on PointNet, and 94.10\% when trained using PointNet++.}
elements.sent-2411-239: Their work was the first to apply PointNet and PointNet++ to spatio-temporal data; however, they did not address hardware deployment nor use a spiking approach, leaving the energy efficiency of the approach unknown.
elements.sent-2411-240: Furthermore, SpikePoint~\cite{renspikepoint , a spiking variant of PointNet++, has been tested on the DVS128 dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-241: SpikePoint uses a surrogate-based backpropagation method, achieving an accuracy of 98.6\% with a GFLOPs value of 0.9, compared to 0.7 for PointLCA-Net.
elements.sent-2411-242: According to the authors, their study used 26,796 training samples and 6,959 test samples, with the test set comprising ``20\% randomly selected from the total samples".
elements.sent-2411-243: In contrast, the DVS128 dataset~\cite{amir2017low used in this study contains 28,606 training samples and 7,408 test samples, with the test data kept entirely separate from the training data.}
elements.sent-2411-244: Given the differences in data partitioning and sample size, directly comparing accuracy may not be fully representative.
elements.sent-2411-245: Additionally, PointLCA-Net is significantly more energy-efficient, with a total energy consumption of 0.065 mJ—approximately 24.23 times lower than that of SpikePoint, which has a reported dynamic energy consumption of 0.82 mJ and a static energy consumption of 0.756 mJ.
elements.sent-2411-246: All energy calculations are based on theoretical estimations and calculations.
elements.sent-2411-247: This study serves as a proof of concept and has significant potential as an algorithm that can be uniformly applied to various spatio-temporal data beyond DVS128, including video and audio.
elements.sent-2411-248: Our focus was on leveraging PointNet and PointNet++ in PointLCA-Net to recognize spatio-temporal signals in an energy-efficient manner that could be mapped onto current neuromorphic hardware, rather than solely enhancing their accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-249: PointLCA-Net currently has limitations in accuracy compared to state-of-the-art methods, and further improvements could be made by exploring alternative architectures for feature extraction, such as Point Transformer~\cite{zhao2021point .}
elements.sent-2411-250: PointLCA-Net does not require any additional training of the PointNet and PointNet++ models used for feature extraction.
elements.sent-2411-251: Once the features are extracted from the training data, a dictionary is constructed and used in the Exemplar LCA-Decoder.
elements.sent-2411-252: While larger dictionary lengths can improve accuracy, they may also create a computational burden for larger datasets.
elements.sent-2411-253: As a result, this opens up new avenues for enhancing computational efficiency and model performance of PointLCA-Net in future studies.
elements.sent-2411-254: In this work, we explored the integration of Exemplar LCA with PointNets for the first time and evaluated its performance on classification tasks using the NMNIST, DVS128, and SHD datasets.
elements.sent-2411-255: We utilized features extracted through PointNet and PointNet++ within an Exemplar LCA encoder-decoder framework, which eliminated the need for training a dictionary as done in the original LCA.
elements.sent-2411-256: This study highlights the potential for further research, including the investigation of alternative architectures such as Point Transformer for even higher performance.
elements.sent-2411-257: Given the energy efficiency potential of PointLCA-Net and its adaptability to various datasets, it presents an opportunity for enhancement in spatio-temporal signal recognition.
elements.sent-2411-258: The author would like to thank Dr. Jack Sampson for his valuable guidance and constructive feedback during the preparation of this manuscript and Harm Munk for his assistance with the image used in Fig. 2.
